items.question: Test coverage measures:
options: what fraction of your code your tests execute
items.answer: what fraction of your code your tests execute
items.explanation: It's an *execution* metric, not a quality metric.

items.question: Which is the stricter signal on branched code?
items.explanation: Branch coverage requires both sides of every conditional to be exercised.

items.question: What does `# pragma: no cover` do?
options: tells coverage.py to ignore the line/block
items.answer: tells coverage.py to ignore the line/block
items.explanation: Use it sparingly and always document why.

items.question: Why is diff-coverage often more useful than total-project coverage as a PR gate?
options: it focuses on the lines this PR added/changed instead of legacy gaps
items.answer: it focuses on the lines this PR added/changed instead of legacy gaps
items.explanation: Total numbers move slowly; risk lives in new code.

items.question: Mutation testing reveals:
options: weak asserts that wouldn't catch a real bug
items.answer: weak asserts that wouldn't catch a real bug
items.explanation: It flips a line and checks whether any test fails.

items.question: Setting a 100% coverage gate on a large codebase tends to:
options: encourage gaming via weak asserts; discourage refactors
items.answer: encourage gaming via weak asserts; discourage refactors
items.explanation: Realistic targets + diff-coverage produce better behaviour.
